paper_title: Accurate and scalable exchange-correlation with deep learning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Density Functional Theory (DFT) underpins much of modern computational chemistry and materials science. Yet, the reliability of DFT-derived predictions of experimentally measurable properties remains fundamentally limited by the need to approximate the unknown exchange-correlation (XC) functional. The traditional paradigm for improving accuracy has relied on increasingly elaborate hand-crafted functional forms. This approach has led to a longstanding trade-off between computational efficiency and accuracy, which remains insufficient for reliable predictive modelling of laboratory experiments. Here we introduce Skala, a deep learning-based XC functional that surpasses state-of-the-art hybrid functionals in accuracy across the main-group chemistry benchmark set GMTKN55 with an error of 2.8 kcal/mol, while retaining the lower computational cost characteristic of semi-local DFT. This demonstrated departure from the historical trade-off between accuracy and efficiency is enabled by learning non-local representations of electronic structure directly from data, bypassing the need for increasingly costly hand-engineered features. Leveraging an unprecedented volume of high-accuracy reference data from wavefunction-based methods, we establish that modern deep learning enables systematically improvable neural exchange-correlation models as training datasets expand, positioning first-principles simulations to become progressively more predictive.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The energy of the electrons in molecules and materials serves as glue between their atoms, determining the stability and properties of the chemical structure. Accurately computing the many-electron energy is therefore essential for predictive modeling across broad spectrum of applications, including assessing whether chemical reaction will proceed, whether candidate drug molecule will bind to its target protein, whether material is suitable for carbon capture, or whether flow battery can be optimized for renewable energy storage. Unfortunately, computing this energy amounts to solving the Schrödinger equation, whose cost scales exponentially with the number of electrons . Density functional theory (DFT)1 provides an exact reformulation that replaces the many-electron wavefunction with the much simpler electron density. Although exact in principle, one component of the total energy the exchange-correlation (XC) functional remains unknown and must be approximated in practical implementations. The role of the XC functional is to capture intricate quantum many-body interactions of electrons using only the electron density, making this universal functional that has the same form for all molecules and materials.2,3 Equipped with formalism4 whose cost scales asymptotically as O(N 3), and supported by practical functional approximations pioneered over several decades,512 DFT has become the computational workhorse in disciplines ranging from (bio)chemistry to catalysis to materials science.13 However, DFT users must still choose from among hundreds of XC functional approximations,11,13,14 often relying on dedicated benchmark studies or experimental results to guide the choice for the application at hand. Crucially, current XC approximations still fall short of the accuracy required to reliably predict experimental outcomes across wide range of chemical systems and properties.11,13,14 Achieving this level of accuracy commonly known as chemical accuracy typically demands errors below 1 kcal/mol for processes abWork done during an internship at Microsoft Research AI for Science aCurrent address: University of Geneva, Department of Computer Science bCurrent address: Mila Quebec AI Institute Extract grid features LDA: only density GGA: with density gradient meta-GGA: with kinetic energy (Skala) Local hybrid: with local exchange Hartree energy, Kohn-Sham kinetic energy, electron-nuclear Coulomb potential, (optional) dispersion correction (cid:90)

(a) Jacobs ladder of density functional approximations16 defines the rungs LDA, GGA and meta-GGA by expanding the set of semi-local features they extract from an electronic density matrix into grid representation. The next rungs, hybrid and double hybrid, extract increasingly expensive wavefunction-based information directly from the density matrix. Skala departs from this ladder by extracting relatively cheap meta-GGA features, and instead gaining expressivity by learning non-local interactions between grid points at manageable and controllable cost. (b) High-level overview of the neural network architecture for the Skala functional. (c) Weighted total mean absolute deviation (WTMAD-2) on the GMTKN5514 benchmark for general main-group thermochemistry, kinetics and non-covalent interactions. Results are shown for popular functionals, including the best-performing ones in each computational cost category. involving making and breaking covalent chemical bonds.13 This means, for example, that in silico screening pipelines for molecule and material discovery often pass too many candidates to the lab, with large fraction failing experimental verification. In addition, lower-cost methods such as force fields and property-guided generative models trained on DFT data inherit these same limitations. The search for general-purpose XC functional that meets chemical accuracy has persisted for over 60 years and is sometimes referred to as the pursuit of the divine functional 15 challenge with profound implications for accelerating scientific discovery. The prevailing approach has been to handcraft functional forms based on limited set of ingredients defined by the so-called Jacobs ladder of DFT;16 see Fig. 1. Like its biblical namesake, it is intended to guide users toward the heaven of chemical accuracy. The ingredients at the lower rungs allow retaining the asymptotic O(N 3) scaling of DFT, but amount to XC functionals that only use (semi-)local information such as the density, its gradient, the Laplacian and the Kohn-Sham kinetic energy density. However, it is well established that the exact XC functional exhibits non-local dependence on the density,3 and in practice lower-rung approximations yield only limited accuracy. To improve accuracy, researchers began introducing non-locality through wavefunction-like ingredients.8,9 While this approach enhances accuracy in many cases, it does not do so for all chemical problems, and it increases the computational complexityi to O(N 4), O(N 5) or higher, thereby defining the higher rungs of the ladder. All XC functionals in common use are built from this hierarchy of Jacobs ladder ingredients, with clear accuracy-cost trade-off. They differ primarily in how these ingredients are combined and the number of parameters involved. The focus on these ingredients is driven by their availability in standard software packages and compatibility with exact constraints, offering practical foundation for building functional approximations. iWe express computational cost using the standard asymptotic scaling with system size, O(f (N )). In practice, however, actual performance depends on algorithmic speedups, hardware optimizations, and prefactors. Therefore, in Sec. 5, we empirically compare the cost of Skala with that of other functionals. As in many other areas of science, machine learning (ML) has been explored as promising approach for developing accurate XC functionals, revealing the challenges and subtleties of this complex learning problem.17 Yet, to date this has not led to meaningful shift in the established accuracy-cost trade-off, and no ML-based functional has seen widespread adoption. There are two interlinked reasons for this. First, high-accuracy data for this complex learning problem are very scarce, as they must be generated using computationally intensive wavefunction methods that require specialized expertise to be used at scale. Second, confined to this low-data regime, the vast majority of efforts have been limited to feeding handcrafted features into machine learning models, whether based on Jacobs ladder ingredients1824 or newly designed descriptors.2528 This approach mirrors classic machine learning strategies prior to the rise of deep learning (DL), which may partly account for the limited impact observed so far. In the absence of sufficient data to power more modern deep representation learning approach, the handful of efforts to move beyond handcrafted features, though promising, have remained focused on model systems or narrowly defined problems.2933 In this work, we introduce Skala, modern deep-learning solution to this long-standing problem, addressing both the scarcity of chemically accurate training data and several fundamental challenges in applying deep learning to exchange-correlation functionals. Using an efficient wavefunction-based protocol,34 we generated training set of approximately 400, 000 high-accuracy energy differences spanning diverse chemistry, augmented by the limited public datasets available at comparable accuracy. We further design neural network architecture capable of learning non-local electronic representations directly from data, while relying only on simple semi-local input features. Skala achieves higher accuracy than leading hybrid functionals across broad main-group chemical space. Notably, this is accomplished using scalable neural-network formulation that retains the asymptotic complexity of semi-local DFT and naturally enables GPU acceleration, effectively decoupling accuracy from computational cost along Jacobs ladder. Skala also matches or exceeds hybrid functionals in predicting equilibrium structures and dipole moments. As additional high-accuracy data covering increasingly diverse regions of chemical space are incorporated in training, we demonstrate that Skala accuracy improves systematically without an increase in computational complexity at inference time. This empirical scaling behaviour motivates the design of Skala as continuously evolving exchange-correlation modelling framework rather than static functional. Unlike the traditional DFT zoo,14 in which new approximations accumulate without retirement of older ones, Skala follows an explicit, versioned development process, with successive releases superseding their predecessors through significant demonstrable gains in accuracy and robustness. In this manuscript, Skala refers to the current best model, Skala-1.1ii, with version labels provided in figures and tables where appropriate to ensure transparency and reproducibility. More broadly, by establishing framework for systematically improvable DFT, this work outlines concrete route towards genuinely predictive accuracy. Achieving such predictive accuracy would remove longstanding bottleneck that has constrained the fields ability to shift the centre of gravity from laboratory-based experimentation towards in silico discovery.

sections.title: 2 Deep learning the XC functional: basic challenges and solutions
sections.content: The success of DFT is based on the Kohn-Sham (KS) formalism,4 which decomposes the total energy functional into components that capture large effects such as the Pauli exclusion principle and long-range classical electrostatics, as well as the remaining unknown term that we aim to learn the XC functional which accounts for smaller but crucial energy contribution due to quantum many-body effects. The XC functional Exc[ρ] maps the electron density ρ(r), non-negative function over three-dimensional space, to scalar value representing the XC energy. In practical implementations of KS DFT, all terms except for the XC functional are evaluated using the density represented in basis set via the density matrix, with atom-centered Gaussian functions being the most commonly used basis functions in chemistry. Focusing on the semi-local functional rungs, the XC energy is instead evaluated using representation of the electron density on large integration grid. For molecules containing up to several hundred atoms, the integration grids typically consist of 104106 points. Since any such grid is necessarily finite representation of the continuous density, the XC functional should have well-defined limit when the grid becomes infinitely dense and show good convergence as the grid is refined.

sections.title: 2.1 The two-stage training protocol
sections.content: The learning problem we address is to obtain an XC functional Eθ xc[ρ] parameterized by neural network with parameters θ that, combined with the other known energy terms, yields accurate total energy predictions. An iiA previous version of this manuscript reported results for Skala -1.0 trained on roughly 150, 000 data points. The present, more accurate version Skala -1.1 is trained on 400, 000 data points and features refined deep-learning architecture and training protocol. 3 important factor in designing training protocol is that, at inference time, Eθ xc[ρ] is not just evaluated once in isolation. Instead, it is used inside self-consistent field (SCF) loop that minimizes the total energy with respect to the density, yielding both the energy minimum and the minimizing self-consistent density ρSCF. This makes the learning problem different from standard regression problem, such as one encounters for instance when training machine-learned force fields.35 While previous ML attempts at learning the XC functional have proposed and analyzed several training protocols to address this challenge,19,22,23,25,30,33 many of them are too computationally demanding for the much larger-scale training considered in this work. Performing SCF calculations for every training example is more expensive than doing single inference call of the model, and this cost becomes prohibitive when considering xc is very poor our large-scale training dataset. Moreover, close to model initialization, the parameterized Eθ estimate of the true XC functional: the SCF procedure would fail to converge for many systems, and for those that do converge, the resulting self-consistent densities and energies would be of poor quality. Starting from such poor predictions risks the functional compensating functional-driven errors with density-driven errors in order to drive down the error between the predicted total energy and the reference energy provided by the high-accuracy wavefunction method. The alternative strategy we adopt is to introduce two-stage training protocol, consisting of pre-training phase and fine-tuning phase. In the pre-training phase the functional is evaluated for fixed input density, bypassing the SCF procedure. This brings the problem closer to standard regression: single forward pass of the model, combined with the other known energy terms, yields total energy prediction for fixed input density features. However, this approach requires reasonable approximation of the true minimizing ground-state density. Producing highly accurate ground-state densities beyond the quality of DFT densities is even more challenging than producing accurate energy labels,3638 and does not scale to our dataset size. Instead, following Kirkpatrick et al. 22, we use approximate B3LYP8,39 densities (not B3LYP energies) during training, which can be generated at scale for our entire dataset.40 This enables us to expose the model to broad range of approximate densities, combined with very accurate wavefunction-level reference energy labels, at reasonable training cost. To predict the total energy, the remaining KS energy components are computed from the B3LYP density and KS orbitals, as detailed in Sec. B.1 of the Supplementary Information. Since reference energies from wavefunction methods are generally more accurate for reaction energy differences than for total energies, the model is trained with reaction energy regression loss rather than total energy loss. After this pre-training phase, the model produces reasonable energy predictions given B3LYP densities, but it has not yet been incentivized to produce accurate self-consistent densities and total energies when deployed inside an SCF procedure. This motivates second, much shorter, fine-tuning phase. In the fine-tuning phase, the model is trained using its own SCF densities, generated on the fly during training. This aims to close the gap between the accuracy achieved when evaluating the functional on the fixed approximate input densities from the pre-training stage, and the accuracy obtained when evaluating the functional on its own SCF densities. Like during pre-training, the energy references we train against originate from wavefunction-level theory. Crucially, this procedure does not require backpropagating through the SCF loop, as described in more detail in Sec. B.5 of the Supplementary Information. During the SCF fine-tuning phase we monitor the aforementioned accuracy gap on holdout validation set, as well as the accuracy of our SCF densities by comparing dipole moments with accurate labels available in the literature.41 As we show in Sec. 4.1, notable empirical observation of our XC learning framework is that, during fine-tuning, both the SCF energies and the SCF electron densities improve simultaneously, suggesting that the increased accuracy in energies is not the result of an error compensation between functional-driven error and density-driven error.

sections.title: 2.2 The XC functional architecture of Skala
sections.content: Several mathematical properties of the XC functional are known, usually referred to as exact constraints.3,10,42,43 Following well-established practice in DFT, we build in some of the most energetically relevant constraints (such as the high-density uniform coordinate scaling, size-consistency, and the Lieb-Oxford lower bound44) by constructing Skala as Eθ xc[ρ] = (cid:19) 1 3 (cid:90) (cid:16) (cid:18) 6 π 3 4 ρ()(r)4/3 + ρ()(r)4/3(cid:17) fθ[x[ρ]](r) dr, (1) where ρ() and ρ() are the densities of the two spin channels and fθ is bounded enhancement factor. The vast majority of previous ML attempts only learned the enhancement factor with local function fθ(x[ρ](r)) of the given hand-designed input features x[ρ](r).1923,2528 In contrast, our DL approach takes inspiration from neural operators,45 and models the enhancement factor as neural functional that learns non-local representations from the input features, hence the distinct notation fθ[x[ρ]](r). The architecture for the enhancement factor, outlined in Fig. 1(b), takes as input the set of density-dependent semi-local features x[ρ] of the standard meta-generalized-gradient approximation (meta-GGA) O(N 3) rung at each integration point r. The first module learns spin-order invariant features, followed by further learned local processing at each position r. Subsequently, in the non-local interactions module, information is exchanged between each grid point and its atoms coarse point (a representative point on coarser, atom-centred grid; see Sec. 7.1), resulting in scalable architecture that preserves the asymptotic computational complexity of semi-local DFT. Finally, the spin-order invariant features and the non-local representations are combined and further processed to predict the enhancement factor fθ[x[ρ]](r), followed by numerical integration using Eq. (1). For more detailed overview of the architecture, the reader is referred to Extended Data Fig. 5 and Sec. 7.1. Importantly, Skalas architecture is designed to have well-defined limit when the grid becomes infinitely dense: once trained, it can be evaluated on different grid types and sizes, and displays convergence behavior similar to traditional functionals, as shown in Fig. 13 of the Supplementary Information. Non-locality on the DFT grid has also been used in prior hand-crafted approaches to model dispersion4648 long-range, subtle yet crucial component of the XC energy, essential for capturing interactions that do not involve the making or breaking of covalent chemical bonds.49 Our focus here is distinct: we target thermochemistry the energetics of forming and breaking covalent bonds and kinetics, namely the energy barriers separating reactants from products. Accordingly, we do not yet attempt to model dispersion explicitly, and instead train our functional using fixed D3 dispersion correction.50,51 Learning dispersion interactions within this framework is deferred to future work. With learned non-locality, Skala eliminates the need for computationally expensive ingredients higher up Jacobs ladder, achieving high accuracy for main-group chemistry given sufficient training data without increasing computational cost. In particular, accuracy in main-group thermochemistry and kinetics has long been dominated by the accuracycost trade-off that defines Jacobs ladder. By disrupting this trade-off, we establish deeplearning approach that is systematically improvable with more data, charting route towards learning the universal XC functional in scalable manner.

sections.title: 2.3 The training data
sections.content: More broadly, our approach mirrors the shift from classic machine learning with hand-designed features to deep representation learning with neural networks.5254 Just as large-scale datasets such as ImageNet55,56 acted as catalysts for deep learning in computer vision57, realizing this approach for DFT demands dataset far exceeding what has been available to date. Given the lack of public datasets at the required scale and accuracy, we have initiated40 large-scale effort to generate and assemble chemically diverse collection of high-accuracy quantum chemistry data, which we term the Microsoft Research Accurate Chemistry Collection (MSR-ACC). All reference energies are obtained with wavefunction methods achieving at least 1 kcal/mol accuracy with respect to experiment, and the collection continues to expand. The current version of MSR-ACC comprises approximately 395,000 energy differences labeled with the high-accuracy W1-F12 and W1w thermochemical protocols,34,58 achieving CCSD(T)/CBS accuracy. Its largest component, in terms of both scale and coverage, consists of roughly 120,000 total atomization energies (TAEs). The remaining datasets span broad range of chemical space, including proton and electron affinities, ionization potentials, reaction pathways, non-covalent clusters, water dimer structures, as well as slightly distorted geometries and conformers derived from selected molecules in the TAE set, in addition to atomic properties. This collection is further augmented with approximately 80,000 energy differences from publicly available datasets of comparable or higher accuracy, covering diverse chemistry such as non-covalent interactions, atomization energies of larger molecules, decomposition energies of mindless structures, and reaction barriers. After removing overlap with test sets based on molecular graphs and reserving dedicated holdout sets, the resulting training set for the current version of Skala (Skala-1.1) comprises about 400,000 energy differences, representing an unprecedented combination of size, accuracy, and chemical diversity.

The accuracy of Skala for general main-group chemistry is readily apparent in Fig. 1, which compares its WTMAD-2 error on GMTKN55 to that of widely used functionals, including the best-performing ones in the first three rungs of Jacobs ladder (up to the hybrid, or O(N 4), rung).ii Skala achieves the lowest overall WTMAD-2 error, surpassing all hybrid functionals despite operating at semi-local cost. detailed breakdown across the 55 iiWe do not include comparisons with local hybrid functionals, which are more computationally demanding than global hybrids in standard implementations and have not yet seen widespread adoption. Examples include the DM21 functional,22 machine-learned local hybrid that reports WTMAD-2 of 3.97 kcal/mol. More recent local hybrids60 incorporate neural networks to learn portions of the functional and achieve WTMAD-2 values down to 2.47 kcal/mol. We also omit the very recent global hybrid functional COACH,61 which is not yet widely available and reports incremental improvements over ωB97M-V. In all these approaches, portions of the GMTKN55 benchmark are included in the training data, and the W4-17 dataset is used in its entirety for training. 6 (a) Ablations on Diet GMTKN55 (b) Analysis of Tc positivity constraint Figure 2: Model and data ablations. (a) Left panel: Accuracy of Skalas non-local architecture compared with its local branch only, trained on the full dataset in Extended Data Table 6. Right panel: Data composition ablation from Extended Data Table 6: set contains thermochemistry, basic properties, distorted geometries and transition metals. Set contains barrier heights and reactions. Set contains NCI and conformations. Details on the specific datasets that belong to A, B, are shown in Extended Data Table 6. In both ablations, for each setting we trained five models using different random seeds (affecting model initialization and data ordering); each dot represents one model instance and bars show the average. Note that for the local models, two seeds yield nearly identical results, causing their dots to overlap. SCF fine-tuning was done for 20,000 steps, and evaluation was performed on the smaller Diet GMTKN55.63 For details on SCF convergence in the ablation studies, see Sec. D.4. (b) The kinetic correlation component Tc of Exc for several atoms, when training on MSR-ACC/TAE2540 only, and when training on the full set. Reference data64,65 obtained by reverse-engineering CCSD densities in pure Kohn-Sham framework for H, He, Ne and Ar are also shown for comparison. individual subsets of GMTKN55 in Table 1 shows that Skala is the best-performing functional on 32 of the 55 subsets, more than all other functionals combined. In particular, Skala outperforms the best hybrid functional across nearly all thermochemistry and kinetics benchmarks, ranging from small to large systems. It is widely assumed that this level of accuracy requires ascending to the hybrid (or even double-hybrid) rung of Jacobs ladder; however, our results indicate that Skala learns the necessary non-local effects directly from data, without recourse to HartreeFock exchange. Moving to atomization energies, we note that all molecular structures in our TAE training set have singlereference electronic structure character, which can be treated accurately with the thermochemical W1-F12 and W1w protocols34,58 based on CCSD(T)/CBS. The test set W4-17, by contrast, is labeled with the higher-level CCSDTQ5-based W4 protocol,58,62 which can also handle the multi-reference cases present in this test set. Training on the large TAE set enables Skala to reach chemical accuracy (MAE 0.92 kcal/mol) on the singlereference subset of W4-17 (183 reactions out of 200), with an overall MAE of 1.23 kcal/mol when we include also the multi-reference cases for which we lack training data (see Extended Data Fig. 8a). In Extended Data Fig. 7, we evaluate Skala on holdout splits of the training data spanning diverse chemical properties, demonstrating strong generalization across broad chemical space. In Sec. of the Supplementary Information, we further assess aspects of practical usability, such as SCF-cycle convergence as reported in Table 3 and grid-size convergence shown in Fig. 13. As expected for deep-learned functional, Skala exhibits slightly less smooth behavior than traditional functionals, but all variations remain within acceptable ranges.

sections.title: 3.1 The importance of learning nonlocal interactions
sections.content: The non-local branch of our architecture is remarkably lightweight Skala comprises just 385, 217 parameters in total, with 331, 265 allocated to the local branch. This compact design is crucial for maintaining scalability. We therefore quantify the performance gains enabled by learned non-locality. In the left panel of Fig. 2a, we compare models that are purely local to models sharing the same non-local architecture as Skala, both on the full training set of Extended Data Table 6. For each setting, we trained multiple instances with different random seeds, and evaluated them on Diet GMTKN55,63 representative subset designed to approximate the WTMAD-2 metric on the full GMTKN55 dataset. This ablation shows the significant improvement enabled by the non-local branch, which reduces the average WTMAD-2 error across 5 seeds by about 50%. 7 The clear performance gap between the local and non-local models confirms that Skala captures essential non-local effects directly from data, without relying on HartreeFock exchange. This finding is further supported by the molecular thermometer recently introduced by Grimme et al. 66 for quantifying effective non-local exchange. As shown in Extended Data Fig. 9a, Skala-1.1 yields an effective non-local exchange of 53.7%, close to the 60% reference value, outperforming several hybrid functionals.66 As another prototypical application where HartreeFock exchange is widely considered essential, we report in Extended Data Fig. 9b barrier heights for SN2 reactions:67 once again, Skala-1.1 achieves accuracy comparable to the best hybrid functionals, with errors roughly 2.5 times smaller than those of the best meta-GGAs.

sections.title: 3.2 Skala’s accuracy improves systematically with training data
sections.content: The right panel in figure 2a reports an ablation study on training data composition, which shows systematic improvement of Skala on Diet GMTKN55 as we add more diverse chemistry in training. We find that training on thermochemistry, basic properties, distortions and transition metals (set A) on average yields GGA-level accuracy on Diet GMTKN55. Adding reactions and barrier heights (set B) brings modest improvement, and the inclusion of non-covalent interactions and conformers (set C) further reduces the WTMAD-2 error (see Extended Data Table 6 for the detailed composition of each set). At each stage, increasing the size and diversity of the training data both lowers the average WTMAD-2 error and reduces the inter-seed variance, reflecting better coverage of the relevant chemical space.

sections.title: 3.3 The emergence of learned physical constraints with training data
sections.content: Exact constraints of the XC functional have been pivotal in guiding the approximations that made DFT practical for thousands of applications in chemistry and materials science.43 Many are ingeniously built in by design,7,10 lending robustness to the functionals that include them. In Skala, we imposed only minimal constraints to maximize model flexibility, making it interesting to explore whether physical constraints can emerge from data. In Fig. 2b, we assess whether the model learns to satisfy the positivity of Tc,42 the kinetic correlation component of Exc. This constraint reflects the physical principle that electron correlation increases the kinetic energy, as electrons move faster to avoid one another due to their mutual Coulomb repulsion. We evaluate θ for several atoms and perform data ablation analysis. When Skala is trained only on the MSR-ACC/TAE25 dataset,40 the constraint is largely violated. In contrast, training on the full dataset leads to consistent satisfaction of the constraint, yielding values of θ not far from values obtained by reverse-engineering CCSD densities.64,65 subtle point is that our functional is evaluated within the generalized Kohn-Sham (GKS) framework, where the kinetic correlation energy is not constrained to match its definition in the pure Kohn-Sham scheme, and may therefore differ from the corresponding exact Tc. meta-GGA within the generalized Kohn-Sham has much more freedom to move energy contributions between the kinetic energy and the XC functional. Notably, with our large training set Skala yields Tc values close to pure Kohn-Sham ones, despite the additional freedom of the GKS framework. For more detailed results and discussion, the reader is referred to Sec. E.4 of the Supplementary Information.

sections.title: 4 Beyond energies: Densities and equilibrium geometries
sections.content: After establishing the accuracy of Skala on energies, we assess the quality of its predicted electron densities via dipole moments and evaluate its predicted equilibrium geometries.

sections.title: 4.1 Densities
sections.content: Recall that the energy error from KS DFT calculation with given XC functional can be decomposed into two components: functional error, which is the error the functional would make if evaluated on the exact density, and density-driven error, which is the error the exact functional would make when evaluated on the self-consistent density of the approximate functional.7173 These two errors can compensate each other,74,75 yielding XC approximations that improve energies by worsening their SCF densities, straying from the path toward the exact functional, quoting Medvedev et al. 76 We pretrain our functional to reproduce accurate wavefunction energy differences when evaluated on the approximate densities ρB3LYP. We then fine-tune it for small number of steps using on-the-fly SCF densities, thereby closing the gap between the accuracy learned on ρB3LYP and that obtained on the self-consistent densities ρSkala produced by Skala, as detailed in Sec. B.5 of the Supplementary Information. To ensure that this SCF fine-tuning does not rely on error compensation, we monitor the quality of the resulting densities by comparing their dipole moments against highly accurate dataset of 151 structures.41 Fig. 3a illustrates this process. sections.title: 4.2 Equilibrium geometries
sections.content: One of the primary uses of DFT is the prediction of equilibrium molecular structures by relaxing the nuclear coordinates to their lowest-energy configuration. We find that when Skala is trained only on equilibrium structures and reaction paths, its predicted equilibrium geometries reach, at best, GGA-level accuracy. Motivated by the idea that Skalas accuracy can be systematically improved by augmenting the training data to target specific regions of chemical space and properties, we generated approximately 20,000 slightly distorted structures from subset of molecules in the MSR-ACC/TAE dataset. For each distorted structure, we computed the energy difference with respect to equilibrium using the accurate W1w protocol,34,58 thereby constructing the MSR-ACC/Distortion dataset. Because the MSR-ACC/TAE equilibrium structures were originally obtained with an approximate functional40 (B3LYP), the resulting W1w labels for these small distortions effectively encode information about minima at CCSD(T)/CBS accuracy. We benchmark geometries optimized with Skala against (semi-)experimental datasets comprising light maingroup bond lengths (LMGB35),68 heavy main-group bond lengths (HMGB11),68 as well as bond lengths and angles for small molecules in the CCse2169 and the CCSD(T)/CBS W4-11-GEOM datasets.70 The results, shown in Table 3c, compare Skala not only with functionals across different rungs of Jacobs ladder, but also 9 (a) (b) Figure 4: Computational cost. (a) Wall-clock time for exchange-correlation integration per SCF step as function of molecular size, measured using GauXC. Only the XC numerical integration is timed: eval_exc_vxc for pure functionals (SVWN5, revPBE, r2SCAN) and eval_exc_vxc plus sn-LinK exact exchange for hybrids (B3LYP, M06-2X). Coulomb matrix construction, diagonalization, and SCF convergence are not included. All computations use the def2-TZVP basis set, GauXCs GM3 integration grid (MuraKnowles radial quadrature, Robust pruning), and unrestricted Kohn-Sham (UKS). D.1 for more information on all molecules. orbitals with the semi-empirical GFN2-xTB method.77 Skala achieves an accuracy on par with, or exceeding, that of the best hybrid functionals. In practical applications, Skala would typically be used to compute both equilibrium structures and reaction energies. In Sec. 3, however, we follow standard practice by evaluating all functionals, including Skala, on fixed accurate geometries. This raises the question of the additional error introduced when functional is used to determine both quantities. This error can be quantified using the GEO78 metric, defined as the energy difference obtained when given functional is evaluated on its own optimized geometry versus reference geometry. For Skala, we find very small average GEO of 0.02 kcal/mol on the W4-11-GEOM dataset. Details of the evaluation protocol are provided in Sec. D.6 of the Supplementary Information, alongside GEO results for other functionals and an ablation study that shows the impact of the MSR-ACC/Distortion training set on geometry accuracy (Sec. E.5). Besides improving structure prediction, the MSR-ACC/Distortion dataset allows Skala to generalize to the prediction of highly strained conformations of larger molecules such as adenosine, benzylpenicillin, and efavirenz,79 as shown in Extended Data Figs. 8b and 8c.

sections.title: 5 Computational Efficiency and Integration into Production Codes
sections.content: The computational cost of quantum chemistry methods is commonly expressed through its asymptotic scaling with system size, O(f (N )), convention we have followed so far in this paper. In practice, the prefactors of that scaling can differ by orders of magnitude between methods, the cost can be dominated by other terms for smaller to medium-sized molecules, and the bottleneck for scaling may be memory rather than compute.80 Moreover, hardware-specific optimizations and algorithmic advances continue to lower the cost in practice. For all these reasons, although our architecture design ensures that Skala has the same asymptotic scaling as meta-GGA semi-local DFT, we have to empirically verify its prefactor and actual cost as system size increases. relevant analogy to clarify why this is crucially important is the following. At the hybrid rung of Jacobs ladder we find both global hybrids and local hybrids. For global hybrids, the XC functional contains fixed fraction of exact exchange evaluated on the basis set, which can be made computationally efficient but lacks universality, as different systems often require different optimal fractions. In contrast, the more flexible local hybrids allow the fraction of exact exchange to be position-dependent, requiring the exchange to be evaluated on the grid. Although both have the same asymptotic scaling, the latter has much larger prefactor, with basic implementations being even more expensive than the double hybrids of the next rung. Despite impressive progress over the last 10 decade,8183 less costly implementations of local hybrids are still rare,84 which has prevented their widespread use so far. For five popular XC functionals (SVWN5, revPBE, r2SCAN, B3LYP, M06-2X) and Skala, spanning semilocal to hybrid functionals, we have measured the cost of the XC numerical integration together with sn-LinK82,8587 exact exchange for hybrid functionals (B3LYP and M06-2X)  (fig. 4)  . This isolates the cost of XC evaluation from the other components of KS calculation, which can vary substantially across different codes and implementations. On GPU, Skala exhibits computational cost within 30% of the meta-GGA functional r2SCAN. On CPU, we observe an approximately twofold prefactor relative to r2SCAN for smaller systems, which decreases toward unity as the cost becomes dominated by atomic orbital evaluation for larger systems. Hybrid functionals remain roughly factor of 36 more expensive than Skala. Having confirmed empirically that the Skala architecture is scalable while retaining the computational cost of semilocal DFT, second barrier to its widespread adoption is its availability in production codes. Many modern electronic structure packages implement standard XC functionals via libraries such as libXC88, which process grid points independently. The grid-level non-locality introduced by Skala makes it incompatible with this paradigm. However, our results suggest that achieving substantially higher accuracy in DFT without increasing computational cost will require enabling deep-learning functionals that learn non-locality, such as Skala, to be readily implemented and optimized within production codes. For quantum chemistry codes based on Gaussian basis sets, the GauXC library87,89 provides practical pathway to support this new class of functionals. GauXC is modern, modular C++ library for the evaluation of exchange-correlation (XC) energies and their derivatives in Gaussian basis sets: given density matrix, it performs all grid operations internally and returns the required integrated quantities in the chosen basis, using efficient, scalable distributed-memory XC and integrators for both CPU and accelerator-based (GPU) architectures. Codes such as Psi490 and CP2K91 are already integrating Skala through GauXC. Another possible pathway, which works for any basis set, is direct integration from the released model checkpoint, and this is used in the integration to PySCF92 and is available to other DFT codes through Python, C/C++, and Fortran interfaces.

sections.title: 6 Conclusions
sections.content: We have presented deep learning-based exchange-correlation functional, Skala, that marks significant step forward in the long-standing quest for general-purpose, chemically accurate and computationally efficient density functional. By assembling large-scale training set, designing training protocol that scales efficiently, and developing non-local architecture with low inference cost, we demonstrate that it is possible to learn non-local quantum mechanical effects from simple semi-local inputs, without sacrificing the favourable scaling of semi-local DFT. Skala outperforms state-of-the-art range-separated hybrid functionals across broad range of main-group chemistry at the cost of semi-local DFT, demonstrating that deep learning can break the traditional accuracy-cost trade-off that has governed XC functional development for decades. As we continue to expand the training dataset to encompass broader range of chemical phenomena, we expect Skala to systematically improve in both accuracy and generality. As first step toward extending beyond main-group chemistry, we have incorporated minimal atomic information for 3d and 4d transition metals into the training set. As preview, we probe Skala on selected transition-metal benchmarks (see Extended Data Table 10), where, even with this minimal training signal, it already performs on par with established functionals, indicating strong transferability; further gains, however, will require substantially expanding the scale and diversity of high-accuracy data for transition-metal chemistry. key challenge in this endeavor will be extending coverage to multi-reference and strongly correlated systems, where generating accurate reference data at scale remains an obstacle that will require new scientific and computational advances to overcome. One of the key advantages of learning an XC functional rather than force field from high-accuracy wavefunction data is that the KS framework inherently captures the dominant energy contributions needed to generalize across unseen elements and larger systems. The XC functional represents smaller correction term, and by embedding minimal set of physical constraints into its design, Skala remains robust: it generalizes with high accuracy to most thermochemistry benchmark sets and, in the worst-case scenario, defaults to the performance of standard semi-local DFT. This makes learning an accurate XC functional compelling strategy for transferring the accuracy of wavefunction methods from small systems to the medium-large ones accessible to DFT. In turn, the learned functional can be used to generate high-quality data for larger systems, enabling the training of force fields and other models that typically require large-scale datasets generated with DFT. This creates cascade of accuracy transfer across scales, with the potential to transform the predictive power of computational chemistry.

sections.title: 7 Methods
sections.content: Here we expand on details of the model architecture and the training data. The Supplementary Information contains further detailed information on the model (appendix A), training details (appendix B), training data (appendix C), evaluation protocols (appendix D) and additional results (appendix E).

sections.title: 7.1 Skala: A model for scalable non-local representation learning
sections.content: Skalas enhancement factor in Eq. (1) is non-local functional modeled with deep neural network that takes as input set of semi-local, density-dependent features x[ρ] from the standard meta-generalized-gradient approximation (meta-GGA) rung, which has O(N 3) scaling, and which are represented on the large, irregular numerical integration grid used in DFT. The challenge here is to design an accurate XC functional that models intricate non-local interactions across the grid in order to achieve the accuracy that is often only attainable by more expensive functionals of higher rung, while maintaining computational cost comparable to functionals from the meta-GGA rung. While naive solution with all-to-all communication across the grid would enable non-local representation learning, it is not scalable design, since the cost of doing so on grids of the order of 104 106 points quickly grows out of control. Instead, Skala introduces second coarse grid with far fewer points,93 which acts as an intermediary layer through which the points on the finer grid can communicate. Extended Data Fig. 5 shows the overall schematic of the neural network architecture. Starting from the input meta-GGA features, the seven semi-local inputs are log-transformed, followed by small multilayer perceptron (MLP) that acts strictly locally on each grid point. The MLP is applied twice, once to each spin-ordering of the transformed features, followed by an averaging operation. This yields spin-symmetrized semi-local hidden representation that serves as input for the rest of the model. By making the hidden layer spin symmetric before feeding it through any non-local computation across the grid, we avoid having to run the more expensive part of the non-local neural network twice, saving computational cost. Before the spin-symmetrized features are passed into the non-local interaction model, they are projected to lower-dimensional hidden vector. Subsequently, the coarse points collect non-local information from the fine grid, analogous to the accumulation of multipole moments. More specifically, for each coarse point, the local hidden features on the atoms integration grid are projected onto product of radial basis functions and spherical harmonics that depend on the distance vector between the coarse and fine points, followed by an integration over the atoms grid points. After downsampling, we apply an equivariant linear mixing and symmetric contraction94 on the coarse features to capture higher-body-order correlations. Using the same product basis of radial and spherical components, we then construct functions that when evaluated on the finer grid yield non-local hidden features on each fine grid point, which are invariant with respect to the Euclidean symmetry. This downsampling-processing-upsampling cycle is repeated through stack of non-local layers, with each layer maintaining the hidden dimensionality through skip connection. The use of atom-centered grids, where each grid point is associated with single atomic center, ensures that the model scales linearly in the number of atoms. Finally, the features from the last non-local layer are processed through purely local MLP and projected down to scalar value per grid point. The scalar value is passed through scaled sigmoid activation function with range between 0 and 2,95 yielding bounded enhancement factor that enforces the Lieb-Oxford lower bound.96 This is substituted into the discretized equivalent of Eq. (1) to yield the predicted Eθ xc[ρ]. In Sec. A.6 we show that the hidden features on the coarse grid can be interpreted as multipole moments, and that the non-local module has the expressivity to model multi-body interactions through the symmetric contraction.94,97 While in principle the non-local module has the ability to approximate non-local interactions independently of where the coarse points are placed, we take advantage of the structure of integration grids typically used in DFT centered around the atomic centers and place the coarse-grid points on such atomic centers. For more details on the neural network architecture, see Sec. in the Supplementary Information.

sections.title: 7.2 Training data
sections.content: Our training data comprise 400k reaction energies (Extended Data Tab. 6) computed at the CCSD(T)/CBS level of theory or higher, as detailed in Sec. in the Supplementary Information. The largest subset of our training data is composed of 120k diverse total atomization energies for general molecules with up to nine non-hydrogen atoms (MSR-ACC/TAE). subset of structures up to five non-hydrogen atoms from this dataset consisting of single stable molecular fragment are released as the MSR-ACC/TAE25 dataset, described in Ehlert et al. 98 As part of MSR-ACC we also include data that goes beyond atomization energies. In particular, we include proton affinities (MSR-ACC/PA), ionization potentials (MSR-ACC/IP), and electron affinities (MSRACC/EA) for the molecules in the MSR-ACC/TAE dataset. For conformational energies, the MSR-ACC/Conf 17 dataset includes all conformers within 10 kcal/mol energy window of the molecules in MSR-ACC/TAE. For intermolecular non-covalent interaction energies, the MSR-ACC/NCI includes equilibrium structures from small van-der-Waals clusters with up to six monomers. Additionally, we include the full potential energy surface of the water dimer in the MSR-ACC/Water subset. The MSR-ACC/Distortion subset includes the structures from MSR-ACC/TAE distorted along their normal modes. To cover kinetics, the MSR-ACC/Reactions dataset comprises elementary steps of reactions of small organic molecules with up to 13 non-hydrogen atoms, including both transition states and points along the reaction pathways. In addition to the molecular sets, we include atomic datasets of electron affinities (EAs) and ionization potentials (IPs) including double and triple IPs for elements up to argon as well as total atomic energies to gauge total energies. We extend this in-house training data with few publicly available datasets. In particular, we add the 14 linear and cyclic carbon clusters from the W4-CC dataset99. We draw on the relatively abundant publicly available data and select five datasets from the NCIAtlas collection (D442x10, SH250x10, R739x5, HB300SPXx10, IHB100x10),100104 and we also include the DES370K non-covalent interaction data computed at CCSD(T)/δCBS(aug-cc-pVQZ) level of theory105 and the NCIBLIND dataset of dissociation curves.106 The reaction dataset BH9 is included with its forward and reverse barriers as well as the full reactions,107 and we include the MB2061 dataset of decomposition energies for artificial closed-shell mindless molecules.108 Furthermore, we include small transition metal systems covering dimer bond energies TMD10109 and DAPD,110 and ionization potentials and spin splittings of atomic systems in 3d4dIPSS.109 Test set subtraction: We removed the overlap with the test sets GMTKN55111 and W4-17112 based on the molecular graphs of all systems with more than two atoms, with the exceptions of W4-CC, MB2061, atomic sets, small transition metal systems, and MSR-ACC/Water. We determine molecular graphs (with undetermined bond order) from the bond model of GFN-FF.113 For all the subsets except the NCI ones and BH9, we removed all reactions that contain any molecule that contains any covalently connected subgraph found in any molecule in W4-17 and GMTKN55 (some molecules in W4-17 and GMTKN55 are not recognized as fully connected by GFN-FF). For the NCI sets and the BH9 sets, we subtract GMTKN55 from the training data by removing all reactions that share the same set of molecules (defined by the GFN-FF graph) with the same stoichiometric ratios. This prevents leakage of W4-17 into the trained model and minimizes the overlap with GMTKN55. After the test set subtraction, we further reserved small subset of the data to be used for model selection. The holdout part of MSR-ACC/TAE is released as part of MSR-ACC/TAE2598 (which is subset of MSR-ACC/TAE). Density features: As explained in Sec. 2, in the pre-training phase we evaluate our model at fixed densities using B3LYP114,115 using the def2-QZVP basis set116 or the ma-def2-QZVP basis set117 if the molecule is part of reaction that contains anions. Using the fixed densities, we compute the relative energy of reaction from the B3LYP total energies by replacing the B3LYP XC energies with the XC energies predicted with our functional. To regularize the model with respect to numerical variations on the grid, we use four distinct radial methods (Treutler, MuraKnowles, Gauss-Chebyshev and Delley) at PySCF118 grid level 1 during pre-training and grid level 3 during fine-tuning, and in both stages we augment training with different partitioning weights as detailed in appendix C.5.

sections.title: 7.3 Data availability
sections.content: The training data are summarized in Extended Data Table 6. Molecular structures from the in-house generated MSR-ACC/TAE dataset consisting of single stable molecular fragment that are not derived from the GDB9 set are released publicly as the MSR-ACC/TAE25 dataset, described in Ehlert et al. 98 The in-house generated datasets MSR-ACC/Conf, MSR-ACC/PA, MSR-ACC/IP, MSR-ACC/EA, MSR-ACC/Distortion, MSR-ACC/NCI, and MSR-ACC/Reactions are not released publicly, but detailed information on their generation protocol is given in Sec. 7.2 and Sec. C. Structures of MSR-ACC/Water are publicly available119 and relabeled in-house. W4-CC,99 D442x10,104 SH250x10,103 R739x5,102 HB300SPXx10,100 IHB100x10,100 MB2061,108 NCIBLIND,106 DES370K,105 and BH9107 are all publicly available. The evaluation benchmark sets W4-17,112 MOR41,120 ROST61,121 MOBH35,122 3dTMV,123 3d4dIPSS,109 DAPD,110 TMD10,109 Wiggle150,124 W1-SN2-BH,125 and GMTKN55,111 the dipole moment evaluation dataset,126 and the geometry optimization datasets LMGB35,127 HMGB11,127 W4-11-GEOM,128 and CCse21129 are all publicly available. The molecular structures for the computational cost results are described in appendix D.1  (Fig. 11)  , and are collected from the following publicly available sources: Grimme,130 S30L,131 HS13L,132 and NCI16L.

sections.title: 7.4 Code availability
sections.content: The Skala model and inference code are available under MIT license at https://github.com/microsoft/skala. The repository contains the PyTorch implementation of the Skala model and its hookups to quantum chemistry packages PySCF,118 GPU4PySCF134 and ASE.135 The Skala model is also served in Azure AI Foundry at https://ai.azure.com/catalog/models/Skala, where the SCF evaluation is implemented using Accelerated DFT136 (inference on GPU) and GauXC.

sections.content: 28 Modeling the exchange-correlation functional In this section we summarize how we model the exchange-correlation functional. We describe the input features and the architecture. A.

sections.title: In theory
sections.content: The foundation of DFT is built on the insight that the ground-state energy of many-electron system can, in principle, be expressed as functional of the electron density alone. As explained in Sec. 2 in the main body of the paper, such functional of the electron density has an unknown component, the exchange-correlation part. Following the Kohn-Sham formalism,142 the ground-state energy of many-electron system in static potential can be written as = min ρ Etot[ρ], Etot[ρ] = (cid:90) v(r)ρ(r) dr + (cid:90) (cid:90) ρ(r)ρ(r) r 1 2 drdr + Ts[ρ] + Exc[ρ], (2) where v(r) in the first term is the external potential due to the nuclei, the second term is the Hartree electrostatic energy, Ts[ρ] is the kinetic energy of system of non-interacting electrons with density ρ and Exc[ρ] is the exchange-correlation energy of an interacting system with density ρ.142 The expression of Exc is unknown and the central challenge then becomes to find an accurate description for it. Exc is functional of the electron density, meaning that we can define it as Exc : L1(R3) R. Exc takes density ρ as input and it outputs scalar Exc[ρ]. There exist many different ways to produce an approximation. Many ML and traditional functionals represent the Exc as an integral of an energy density, as follows: Eθ xc[ρ] = (cid:19) 1 3 (cid:90) (cid:16) (cid:18) 6 π 3 4 ρ()(r)4/3 + ρ()(r)4/3(cid:17) fθ[x[ρ]](r) dr, (3) where fθ is learnable function of set of features x[ρ] called the enhancement factor. When fθ = 1, the remaining terms reduce to the Local Density Approximation (LDA) exchange functional.143 In Skala, the enhancement factor fθ is parameterized by deep neural network, whose architecture is explained in detail in Sec. A.3. This particular form of Exc, written as learnable enhancement factor times the LDA exchange energy density, is mainly designed to make it easier to enforce properties that the exact Exc functional is known to satisfy, such as the high-density uniform coordinate scaling, size consistency, and the Lieb-Oxford lower bound.144 A.

sections.title: In practice
sections.content: Theoretically, the density ρ is function R3 R. However, in practice, we work with discretized version. Similar to Ref. [145149], we choose to discretize the density features by evaluating them on set of points {ri R3, = 1, . . . , G} that are defined by classical integration grid. An integration grid is set of points ri R3 (effectively, point cloud in R3) and associated weights wi used to numerically approximate spatial integrals, such as the exchange-correlation energy and its potential. We refer to Ref. [150152] for details on such integration grids. This representation of the density evaluated on point cloud in R3 has the advantage of being independent of the basis set. Following the discretization, Exc is therefore approximated as Exc[ρ] (cid:18) 6 π 3 4 (cid:19) 3 (cid:88) i=1 (cid:16) ρ()(ri)4/3 + ρ()(ri)4/3(cid:17) fθ[x[ρ]](ri)wi. Among non-local effects, dispersion is very long-range and traditional functionals do not model it directly. Instead, post-correction is typically applied. As we train on B3LYP densities, we train with its D3 correction156,157 as part of the total energy, and focus on learning the other shorter-range non-local effects. In this section, we introduce mechanism to pass on non-local information with the aid of some chosen helper nodes referred to as coarse points. Extension to -body interaction. Theoretically, one could generalize the theorem above to -body interactions and adapt the non-local layer accordingly.159,161 A.7 Related work The theoretical foundation developed in this section is firmly based on the framework of the atomic cluster expansion (ACE),161,163 which enables the systematic construction of complete descriptor of the atomic environment. Specifically, the expressivity of message passing can be enhanced by increasing the basis set size parameters ℓ and c, as well as the correlation order of the interaction (ν = 3 in our case).159 In the ACE framework, the atomic center directly receives the message. In contrast, our approach accumulates spherical tensor features at coarse points, serving both as computational aid and as means to enrich modeling capacity. These aggregated features are then transmitted back to the integration grid points. This mechanism is reminiscent of numerical techniques such as the fast multipole method (FMM),164 where, for example, the Laurent expansion ϕc can be used to approximate the long-range two-body Coulomb kernel (r1, r2) = 1/r1 r2. similar strategy is employed in Gao et al. 165, where nuclei-centric descriptors of the total density are constructed. Their work further processes the coarsened features using message-passing layers within neural force field framework,166 enabling direct graph-level readout. However, in our preliminary experiments, we observed that this approach can lead to significant overfitting when evaluated on the Diet GMTKN55 benchmark. Other studies have also leveraged coarsened features in various contexts, such as modeling protein binding sites167 and compressing input signals in fluid dynamics simulations.168 From theoretical standpoint, learning the enhancement factor corresponds to learning mapping between functional spaces. In recent machine-learning literature, neural operators have emerged as powerful tools for this purpose.169 These models learn operators that map input functions to output functions and can be evaluated with arbitrary discretization of the domain. In particular, our non-local layer shares similarities with low-rank kernel neural operators,169 which retain rich functional expressivity while reducing computational cost. We tailored the non-local layer design to better suit the structure of the atomic grids, as the irregularity limits the applicability of most standard techniques from the neural operator literature.

sections.content: B.1 Training objective The objective of training is to minimize regression loss on reaction energies, which linearly combines molecular total energies. The total energy of molecule is computed as Eθ tot[M ] = Etotxc[M ] + Eθ xc[ρ] (29) where Etotxc is the total energy minus the exchange-correlation component, calculated using the B3LYP functional.170,171 This is precomputed. Notably, this quantity contains the D3 dispersion energy. We calculate the reaction energy as weighted sum of the stoichiometric coefficients {cM } for reaction
